title: Make It Yours — Fine-Tuning, LoRA and PEFT
subtitle: How to bend a giant model to your will without setting your budget on fire
theme: Three ways to specialize a model — and how to choose the cheapest one that works.
learningObjectives: Explain the difference between full fine-tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)
Build clear intuition for why LoRA works — low-rank updates and frozen weights
Understand QLoRA and why a 70B model can be tuned on a single consumer GPU
Apply the decision triangle: prompt vs RAG vs fine-tune for any given problem
Prepare clean instruction data and avoid catastrophic forgetting
Estimate the real cost — compute, data, and maintenance — of owning a tuned model
storyHook: In 2021 a small team at Microsoft Research had a problem: GPT-3 was magnificent and utterly immovable. Adapting it meant retraining 175 billion parameters — a feat costing millions and weeks of GPU time. They asked a heretical question: what if you never touch the original weights at all, and instead bolt on a tiny, almost insultingly small patch? That patch was called LoRA, and it quietly became the reason your startup can own a custom model today instead of renting OpenAI's.

slides.title: The Sculptor and the Block of Marble
slides.body:
## Three ways to get the statue you want

Imagine a foundation model — GPT-4, Llama 3, Claude — as an enormous, already-carved block of marble. Someone spent **tens of millions of dollars** and a continent of electricity sculpting it from raw stone (the entire internet). It already knows grammar, code, history, sarcasm, the boiling point of water, and how to write a sonnet about quarterly revenue.

Now you want it to do **your** thing — speak in your brand voice, classify your support tickets, write SQL for your weird schema, draft contracts in your firm's style. You have three tools:

1. **Prompting** — you whisper instructions and examples into its ear at runtime. Nothing about the statue changes; you just describe what you want, every single time.
2. **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** — you hand the statue a stack of reference cards right before it answers. It reads your documents on the fly. (That's tomorrow — Day 11.)
3. **Fine-tuning** — you take a chisel to the marble itself. You permanently change the model's weights so the behavior is *baked in*.

Today is about that third tool — the chisel. And the great revelation of the last few years is this: **you almost never need to re-carve the whole statue. You just need to add a thin, removable layer of clay on top.** That layer is called a LoRA adapter, and it changed everything.
slides.speakerNotes: Open by physically miming carving marble. The whole day hinges on the marble-vs-clay metaphor — keep returning to it. Ask the class: 'If you wanted a model to write emails in YOUR personal style, would you prompt it, give it documents, or change its brain? Hold that answer — by the end of today you'll know which is right and why.'
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, the default enterprise instinct has flipped: teams reach for prompting and RAG first, and fine-tune only when behavior — not knowledge — is the gap. Fine-tuning is now a specialist tool, not a starting point.

slides.title: Full Fine-Tuning — The Sledgehammer
slides.body:
## What 'full fine-tuning' actually means

Full fine-tuning takes the pretrained model and continues training it on your data — but this time **every weight in the network is allowed to change.** You run your examples through, compute the loss, backpropagate, and nudge all the parameters.

For a 7-billion-parameter model, that means:

- **7B trainable parameters**, each needing gradients and optimizer state.
- The classic rule of thumb: with the Adam optimizer in 16-bit, you need roughly **~16 bytes per parameter** (weights + gradients + two optimizer moments). For 7B params that's **~112 GB of GPU memory** just for the optimizer math — before you even add activations.
- A 70B model? You're now talking a **multi-GPU cluster** and a serious cloud bill.

### When the sledgehammer is the right tool

Full fine-tuning still wins when you need to **deeply re-specialize** a model — a different language, a different modality, a fundamentally new behavior the base model never learned. Frontier labs do it. Most companies, in practice, do not.

### The two hidden taxes

1. **Catastrophic forgetting** — push hard on narrow data and the model can *forget* its general abilities. Teach it only legal contracts and it may suddenly write worse Python.
2. **Storage and ops** — every fine-tuned variant is a *full copy* of the model. Ten use cases = ten 14 GB checkpoints to store, serve, and version. It does not scale.

This pain is exactly the vacuum that PEFT rushed in to fill.
slides.speakerNotes: Drive home the memory math — people underestimate it wildly. Use the line: 'The model weights are the cheap part. The optimizer state is the fat tax.' Ask: 'Why would changing every weight on narrow data make a model DUMBER at things it used to be good at?' Let them reason toward forgetting.
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, full fine-tuning of frontier-scale models remains the province of well-funded labs and a few enterprises with proprietary data moats; for everyone else it's economically irrational versus PEFT plus RAG.

slides.title: The Heresy That Worked — LoRA's Big Idea
slides.body:
## What if the *change* is small, even when the model is huge?

Here's the insight behind **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)**, from Hu et al., 2021.

When you fine-tune, you're computing an update to a weight matrix — call it **ΔW**. For a layer with a 4096×4096 matrix, ΔW has ~16.7 million numbers. Multiply across all layers and you're back to billions of changes to store.

The LoRA team conjectured something bold: **the *change* you actually need has low intrinsic rank.** In plain English — the meaningful adjustment lives in a small number of directions, even though the matrix is enormous. You don't need a full 4096×4096 update; you need a thin sketch of it.

### The trick: factor the update into two skinny matrices

Instead of learning the full ΔW (4096×4096), LoRA learns two small matrices:

```
ΔW  ≈  B × A

  A : shape (r × 4096)   <- skinny
  B : shape (4096 × r)   <- skinny
  r : the 'rank', often just 8, 16, or 64
```

With **r = 8**, instead of ~16.7M numbers you train **2 × (8 × 4096) ≈ 65,500** numbers — a **~250x reduction** for that layer.

### The genius move: freeze the original

The original weight **W stays frozen.** At inference the model computes:

```
output = W·x  +  (B·A)·x
         ^^^^      ^^^^^^^
      frozen     your tiny learned adapter
```

Because W never changes, you **can't damage the base model**, you can train on a fraction of the hardware, and — crucially — your entire 'custom model' is just those tiny A and B matrices: often a **few megabytes** instead of 14 gigabytes.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the intellectual heart of the day. Slow down. The 'low rank' idea is the one thing they must leave understanding. Analogy: 'A 4K photo is millions of pixels, but I can describe a sunset to you in twenty words. The full update is the 4K photo; LoRA is the twenty-word description that's good enough.' Ask: 'What does freezing W buy us, besides speed?' (Answer: safety from forgetting, and swappable adapters.)
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026, LoRA is the default fine-tuning method across the open ecosystem — Hugging Face PEFT, Unsloth, Axolotl, and every cloud tuning service ship it as the standard path.

slides.title: Adapters You Can Hot-Swap
slides.body:
## One base model, many personalities

Because a LoRA adapter is tiny and the base stays frozen, you get a superpower: **swap behaviors like changing a lens on a camera.**

```
                 ┌────────────────────────┐
                 │   FROZEN BASE MODEL     │
                 │   (e.g. Llama-3-8B)     │
                 │   loaded ONCE in memory │
                 └───────────┬────────────┘
                             │
        ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
        │                    │                     │
   ┌────▼─────┐        ┌─────▼──────┐        ┌─────▼──────┐
   │ LoRA: SQL │        │ LoRA: Legal│        │ LoRA: Brand│
   │  (8 MB)   │        │  (8 MB)    │        │  Voice(8MB)│
   └───────────┘        └────────────┘        └────────────┘
     "write me           "draft this           "reply like
      a query"            clause"               our support"
```

### Why this matters operationally

- **Storage**: Ten use cases = one 16 GB base + ten ~8 MB adapters. Not ten 16 GB copies.
- **Serving**: Modern servers (e.g. vLLM with multi-LoRA) keep the base in GPU memory once and dynamically attach the right adapter per request. You can serve hundreds of fine-tuned 'models' on one box.
- **Safety & rollback**: A bad adapter is deleted in seconds. The base is untouched. Compare that to a botched full fine-tune.
- **Merging (optional)**: For latency-critical paths you can *merge* an adapter back into the weights (`W' = W + B·A`) to get zero inference overhead — at the cost of losing the swappability.

This is the architectural reason PEFT didn't just save money — it **changed how products are built.** A single deployment can wear many faces.
slides.speakerNotes: This slide is the 'aha, it's a business advantage, not just a math trick' moment. Emphasize multi-LoRA serving — it's how SaaS products offer per-customer fine-tuned models cheaply. Ask the class: 'If you ran a CRM company with 5,000 customers, how would you give each one a model tuned to their data?' (Per-tenant LoRA adapters.)
slides.marketConnection: Per-tenant LoRA serving is a quiet workhorse of 2026 vertical SaaS — companies offer 'a model tuned to you' by attaching a small adapter per customer over a shared frozen base, a model economically impossible with full fine-tuning.

slides.title: QLoRA — Tuning a 70B Model on One GPU
slides.body:
## Squeeze, then patch

LoRA shrinks *what you train*. **QLoRA** (Dettmers et al., 2023) shrinks *what you store in memory* — and together they border on magic.

The idea: load the frozen base model in **4-bit quantization** (each weight stored in roughly 4 bits instead of 16), then attach LoRA adapters trained in higher precision on top.

```
  Base weights:   4-bit, frozen        <- tiny memory footprint
  LoRA adapters:  16-bit, trainable    <- the only thing learning
```

### The headline result

QLoRA made it possible to fine-tune a **65B-parameter model on a single 48 GB GPU** — something that previously demanded a small cluster. Suddenly, a grad student or a two-person startup could specialize a frontier-class open model on hardware they could actually afford.

### Key innovations that made it work

- **4-bit NormalFloat (NF4)** — a quantization format tuned for the bell-curve distribution of neural net weights, preserving accuracy better than naive 4-bit.
- **Double quantization** — even the quantization constants get quantized, shaving more memory.
- **Paged optimizers** — spill optimizer state to CPU RAM during memory spikes, so you don't crash on long sequences.

### The trade-off, stated honestly

4-bit base weights introduce a little noise. For most instruction-tuning and style/format tasks, the quality loss is negligible. For tasks demanding maximum precision, plain LoRA (or full fine-tuning) on higher-precision weights may edge it out. QLoRA's gift is **access** — it democratized ownership of custom models.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame QLoRA as the great democratizer: 'Before this, fine-tuning a 65B model was a rich-lab activity. After this, it was a weekend project.' Don't drown them in NF4 internals — the takeaway is 4-bit frozen base + small trainable adapter = consumer-GPU fine-tuning. Ask: 'What do we trade away for fitting it on one GPU?' (A little precision from quantization noise.)
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, QLoRA-style 4-bit tuning is the standard on-ramp for teams fine-tuning open models like Llama, Mistral, and Qwen on rented single-GPU instances or even high-end workstations.

slides.title: The Decision Triangle — Prompt vs RAG vs Fine-Tune
slides.body:
## The single most important slide of the day

Most teams reach for fine-tuning when they should reach for something cheaper. Here is the mental model that prevents that mistake.

```
                    PROMPTING
                  (change INPUT)
                   /          \
                  /            \
                 /              \
                /                \
          RAG  ------------------  FINE-TUNING
     (inject KNOWLEDGE)        (change BEHAVIOR)
```

### The one question that decides it: *is your gap KNOWLEDGE or BEHAVIOR?*

| Your problem | Best tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Model doesn't know your private/recent facts | **RAG** | Knowledge changes constantly; retrieval keeps it fresh. Fine-tuning bakes facts in and they go stale. |
| Model doesn't follow a specific *format* or *style* reliably | **Fine-tune (LoRA)** | Behavior/format is a pattern, not a fact — perfect for adapters. |
| You just need a one-off behavior or quick test | **Prompting** | Zero training cost; iterate in seconds. |
| Niche task, thousands of examples, need consistency + lower token cost | **Fine-tune** | Bakes the instruction in so prompts get shorter and cheaper. |
| Facts AND a specialized voice | **RAG + LoRA together** | Retrieve the facts, let the adapter shape the delivery. |

### The golden rule

> **Fine-tune for *behavior and form*. Use RAG for *knowledge and facts*. Prompt first to find out if you even have a problem.**

The classic anti-pattern: a company fine-tunes a model on its product docs to 'make it know our product,' the docs update next quarter, and now they have a confidently wrong, expensive-to-refresh model. That was a **RAG problem wearing a fine-tuning costume.**
slides.speakerNotes: This is the slide they'll photograph. Make them recite the golden rule back. Run a quick poll with 3 scenarios: (1) 'Answer questions about our 900-page employee handbook' (RAG). (2) 'Always reply in a friendly, on-brand support tone' (fine-tune). (3) 'Summarize this one email' (prompt). Make them argue.
slides.marketConnection: The 'RAG-first, fine-tune-for-behavior' doctrine is the consensus best practice across enterprise AI teams in 2026 — and the most common cause of wasted AI budgets is still teams fine-tuning to inject knowledge that should have been retrieved.

slides.title: Garbage In, Confidently-Wrong Out — Data Prep
slides.body:
## A fine-tuned model is a mirror of its data

The model will faithfully learn whatever pattern is in your examples — including your mistakes, your typos, and your inconsistent formatting. **Data quality, not data quantity, is the lever.** A clean 1,000-example set routinely beats a messy 100,000-example set.

### The shape of instruction-tuning data

Most modern fine-tuning uses a chat/instruction format. The model learns the mapping from a prompt to your *ideal* response:

```json
[
  {
    "messages": [
      {"role": "system", "content": "You are Acme's support agent. Warm, concise, never over-promise."},
      {"role": "user", "content": "My order hasn't shipped and I'm furious."},
      {"role": "assistant", "content": "I'm so sorry for the delay — that's frustrating. I've checked order #_, it ships within 24h, and I've added expedited delivery at no charge. Here's your tracking link the moment it's live."}
    ]
  }
]
```

### Checklist for a dataset that actually works

- **Consistency** — same format, same style, same persona across every example. The model averages your behavior; mixed signals produce mush.
- **Cover the distribution** — include the edge cases, the angry users, the weird inputs. The model only learns what it sees.
- **Quality over volume** — curate ruthlessly. Remove duplicates, contradictions, and any 'meh' responses you wouldn't be proud to ship.
- **Match production** — train on inputs that look like what real users will send, not idealized lab prompts.
- **Hold out a test set** — keep 10-20% unseen so you can measure, not vibe-check.
- **Mind the leakage and the legality** — no PII you shouldn't keep, no copyrighted text you can't use, no test data bleeding into training.

### How much do you need?

For LoRA-style behavior/format tuning, **a few hundred to a few thousand excellent examples** often suffice. You're nudging an already-brilliant model, not teaching it to read.
slides.speakerNotes: Hammer 'consistency.' The single most common failure is mixing tones/formats and wondering why output is inconsistent. Show the JSON and point out the system message persona. Ask: 'If 5% of your training examples were sarcastic by accident, what happens?' (The model becomes randomly sarcastic — it learns your noise.)
slides.marketConnection: In 2026, 'data-centric' tuning dominates: tooling like synthetic-data pipelines and LLM-as-judge filtering exists precisely because curated quality, not raw volume, is what separates a useful fine-tune from a confidently broken one.

slides.title: Catastrophic Forgetting and Other Ways It Goes Wrong
slides.body:
## The model that learned legalese and forgot how to count

Picture a team that fine-tunes a capable open model on 50,000 contract clauses to build a legal assistant. It works beautifully — for contracts. Then someone asks it to add up three invoice line items and it fumbles arithmetic it used to nail. They didn't *add* a skill; they **overwrote** part of the model's general competence. That's **catastrophic forgetting** — narrow, aggressive training erodes broad ability.

### Why LoRA is naturally resistant

Remember: with LoRA the **base weights are frozen.** The general knowledge physically cannot be overwritten — it lives in W, and you only ever add a small B·A patch. This is a deeply underrated reason PEFT is safer than full fine-tuning, not just cheaper.

### The rogues' gallery of fine-tuning failures

- **Catastrophic forgetting** — model loses general skills. *Mitigation:* prefer LoRA; keep learning rates modest; mix in some general data; train fewer epochs.
- **Overfitting** — model memorizes your examples and parrots them instead of generalizing. *Mitigation:* watch the held-out loss; stop early; don't over-train.
- **The 'sycophant from bad data'** — train on lazy or wrong answers and you teach it to produce lazy, wrong answers, confidently.
- **Format lock-in** — over-tune on one rigid format and it can't adapt when you need a different one.
- **Eval blindness** — no test set means you're flying on vibes. You'll ship regressions you never measured.

### The discipline that saves you

Before/after, on a **fixed test set**: measure your target task *and* a few general-capability checks. If the target went up but general ability dropped, you over-chiseled. Back off.
slides.speakerNotes: Tell the legal-assistant story like a cautionary tale — it lands. The key teaching point: LoRA's frozen base is a SAFETY feature, not just an efficiency one. Ask: 'How would you even KNOW your model got worse at general tasks?' (You'd only know if you tested for it — most teams don't, and that's the trap.)
slides.marketConnection: Forgetting and silent regressions are why 2026 production teams treat fine-tuning like software releases — with eval suites, canary deployments, and rollbacks — rather than a one-shot 'train and pray' event.

slides.title: What Does It Actually Cost to Own a Model?
slides.body:
## The bill has more line items than the GPU

People price fine-tuning as 'a few hours of GPU rental.' The compute is often the *smallest* number. Here's the honest ledger.

### 1. Compute (often surprisingly cheap with PEFT)

A LoRA/QLoRA run on a single rented GPU for a few-thousand-example dataset can cost **tens of dollars, not thousands.** This is the part everyone fixates on, and it's the part that barely matters.

### 2. Data (the real expense)

Curating, cleaning, labeling, and reviewing high-quality examples is **human time** — the dominant cost of any serious fine-tune. Budget for it honestly.

### 3. Evaluation

Building the test set and the eval harness to *prove* the model improved (and didn't regress) is real engineering work — and non-negotiable.

### 4. Serving & maintenance (the cost everyone forgets)

- Hosting your own model means **you** now run inference infra, GPUs, autoscaling, uptime, and on-call.
- Every base-model upgrade may mean **re-tuning** to stay current. Your fine-tune is frozen in time; the frontier is not.
- Versioning, monitoring, drift detection — this is an ongoing operational commitment, not a project.

### The build-vs-rent question

| | Fine-tune & self-host | Use a frontier API + prompt/RAG |
|---|---|---|
| Upfront effort | High (data, training, eval) | Low |
| Per-call cost at scale | Potentially much lower | Pay per token |
| Data privacy / control | Maximum (it's yours) | Depends on provider terms |
| Keeping up with the frontier | You must re-tune | Provider upgrades for you |
| Best when | High volume, stable task, privacy needs, behavior gap | Fast iteration, knowledge gaps, small/variable volume |

The sweet spot for owning a tuned model: **a stable, high-volume, behavior-shaped task where privacy or per-token economics justify the ops burden.**
slides.speakerNotes: Crush the myth that fine-tuning is mainly a GPU cost. Say it plainly: 'The GPU is cheap. The data and the babysitting are what cost you.' Walk the build-vs-rent table slowly. Ask: 'What happens to your carefully fine-tuned model when a base model twice as good drops next quarter?' (You may have to redo it — that's the maintenance tax.)
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026 the calculus often favors prompt+RAG on a frontier API for most teams, with fine-tuning reserved for high-volume stable workloads, strict data-residency needs, or shaving cost at massive scale — because frontier models keep leaping ahead, stranding yesterday's fine-tunes.

slides.title: A Real LoRA Run, End to End
slides.body:
## From raw examples to a swappable adapter

Here's the whole arc, deliberately simplified, so the *shape* of the workflow is unmistakable. (Exact APIs change; the steps don't.)

```python
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments, Trainer

# 1) Load a base model in 4-bit (QLoRA-style) — frozen
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    load_in_4bit=True,        # squeeze memory
    device_map="auto",
)

# 2) Describe the LoRA adapter — only THIS trains
lora = LoraConfig(
    r=16,                     # the 'rank' — small = cheap
    lora_alpha=32,            # scaling of the update
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],  # which layers get adapters
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora)

model.print_trainable_parameters()
# -> trainable: ~6M  |  total: ~8B  |  trainable%: 0.07%
#    You are training less than one-tenth of one percent.

# 3) Train on your cleaned instruction dataset
trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=TrainingArguments(
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        num_train_epochs=3,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        output_dir="acme-support-lora",
    ),
    train_dataset=clean_dataset,   # the curated JSON from slide 7
)
trainer.train()

# 4) Save ONLY the adapter — a few megabytes, not gigabytes
model.save_pretrained("acme-support-lora")
```

### Read the punchline in the comments

`trainable%: 0.07%`. That line *is* the entire lesson of PEFT: you froze a brilliant 8-billion-parameter brain and taught it your job by training **six million** numbers — small enough to email. Then you saved an adapter you can hot-swap, version, delete, or merge.

That's the chisel that became a sticker.
slides.speakerNotes: Walk the four steps out loud; you don't need them to memorize the API. Land hard on the `trainable%: 0.07%` line — circle it. Tie back to slide 3's math: rank 16 on q/v projections = a few million params. Ask: 'Which line makes this fit on one GPU?' (load_in_4bit.) 'Which line makes it cheap to train?' (the LoraConfig r=16.)
slides.marketConnection: This exact PEFT/transformers/4-bit stack — plus higher-level wrappers like Unsloth and Axolotl that compress it to a config file — is what most 2026 teams actually run when they fine-tune open models.

slides.title: Recap — The Chisel, The Clay, The Choice
slides.body:
## What you now own

### The three tools, ranked by when to reach for them
1. **Prompt first** — change the input. Free, instant, often enough.
2. **RAG for knowledge** — inject fresh facts at runtime (tomorrow's whole story).
3. **Fine-tune for behavior** — bake in style, format, and consistency.

### The fine-tuning ladder, cheapest first
- **Prompting** → no training.
- **LoRA** → freeze the giant, train a thin low-rank patch (~0.1% of params).
- **QLoRA** → LoRA on a 4-bit base, so it fits on one GPU.
- **Full fine-tuning** → re-carve everything; reserved for deep re-specialization with serious resources.

### The ideas that matter
- The update you need is **low-rank** — small even when the model is huge. That's why LoRA works.
- **Freezing the base** gives you safety (no catastrophic forgetting), swappable **adapters**, and tiny storage.
- **Data quality beats quantity** — the model is a mirror of its examples.
- **The GPU is the cheap part.** Data, evaluation, and maintenance are the real cost.
- **Match the tool to the gap:** knowledge → RAG; behavior → fine-tune; unsure → prompt and find out.

> If you remember one sentence: **Fine-tune to change how the model *behaves*, not what it *knows* — and use the smallest chisel that does the job.**
slides.speakerNotes: Close the loop on the marble metaphor: 'We came to carve marble and discovered we mostly need a sticker we can peel off.' Have the class state the decision rule one more time. Tease that tomorrow we solve the 'knowledge' side of the triangle properly — and that it's the most commercially important day of the course.
slides.marketConnection: The 2026 enterprise reality: most value comes from prompt + RAG on strong base/frontier models, with LoRA/QLoRA as a precise, cost-effective scalpel for behavior — and full fine-tuning as the rare heavy artillery.

slides.title: Quick Quiz — Prove You've Got It
slides.body:
## Five questions. No peeking.

**1. A bank wants its assistant to answer questions about policies that change monthly. Fine-tune or RAG?**
> **RAG.** This is a *knowledge* gap, and the knowledge changes constantly. Fine-tuning would bake in facts that go stale immediately.

**2. In LoRA, what happens to the original model weights during training?**
> They are **frozen**. You only train two small low-rank matrices (A and B) that form an additive patch B·A. This is why LoRA resists catastrophic forgetting.

**3. Why can QLoRA fine-tune a 65B model on a single GPU when full fine-tuning needs a cluster?**
> Because QLoRA loads the **frozen base in 4-bit** (tiny memory footprint) and only trains small high-precision **LoRA adapters** — slashing both memory and trainable parameters.

**4. A team fine-tuned a model on support tickets and now it writes worse code. What happened, and how does LoRA help?**
> **Catastrophic forgetting** from full/aggressive fine-tuning overwriting general skills. LoRA helps because the base weights stay frozen, so general competence can't be erased.

**5. True or false: the GPU rental is usually the biggest cost of fine-tuning.**
> **False.** Compute with PEFT is often cheap (tens of dollars). The real costs are **data curation, evaluation, and ongoing serving/maintenance**.

### One to argue about
Your startup serves 2,000 business customers and wants each to get a model 'tuned to their data.' Sketch the cheapest architecture. *(Hint: one frozen base + per-customer LoRA adapters + multi-LoRA serving.)*
slides.speakerNotes: Run as a live cold-call or table competition. Question 1 and 4 are the ones that separate real understanding from memorization — linger there. End on the 2,000-customer design challenge; let two tables pitch competing architectures before revealing the per-tenant LoRA answer. This bridges perfectly into Day 11.
slides.marketConnection: These exact judgment calls — RAG vs fine-tune, how to serve many tuned variants cheaply — are the daily decisions of AI engineering teams in 2026, and getting them right is worth real money.

keyTakeaways: Fine-tune to change BEHAVIOR (style, format, consistency); use RAG to change KNOWLEDGE (facts) — matching the tool to the gap is the whole game.
LoRA works because the needed weight update is low-rank: freeze the giant base and train two skinny matrices (~0.1% of parameters).
Freezing the base is a safety feature — it prevents catastrophic forgetting and lets you hot-swap tiny, mergeable adapters.
QLoRA loads the frozen base in 4-bit so a 65B model fits on a single GPU — it democratized model ownership.
Data quality beats quantity: a clean few-thousand-example set, consistent in style and format, outperforms a messy huge one.
The GPU is the cheap part — data curation, evaluation, and serving/maintenance are the real and recurring costs.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, the enterprise default has settled into a clear pattern: start with prompting, add RAG for knowledge, and reach for LoRA/QLoRA only when the gap is behavioral — a brand voice, a strict output format, a niche task needing rock-solid consistency or lower per-call cost at scale. Full fine-tuning of frontier-scale models remains a specialist move for well-resourced labs and enterprises with genuine data moats. The open ecosystem (Hugging Face PEFT, Unsloth, Axolotl) has made a QLoRA run a config-file affair, and multi-LoRA serving (e.g. vLLM) powers a wave of vertical SaaS offering per-customer tuned models over a single shared base. The recurring lesson teams keep relearning: because frontier models leap ahead every few months, a fine-tune frozen on last year's base can become a liability — so fine-tune deliberately, for behavior, with eval suites and a rollback plan.
homework: Pick a real task you'd love an AI to do for you or your team. Write one paragraph answering: (1) Is the gap KNOWLEDGE or BEHAVIOR? (2) Which tool fits — prompt, RAG, LoRA, or full fine-tune — and exactly why? (3) If you'd fine-tune, sketch what 10 of your training examples would look like (input → ideal output) and name the single biggest risk (forgetting? stale facts? bad data?). Bring it to Day 11 — we'll pressure-test your knowledge-vs-behavior call against the RAG you're about to learn.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow we build the model a memory. You'll learn how words become coordinates in a vast 'meaning space,' how a vector database finds the right needle in a million-document haystack in milliseconds, and how RAG lets an AI cite your private knowledge without ever being retrained. It's the most enterprise-relevant day of the entire course — the one that turns a clever chatbot into a trustworthy expert.
